documentation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-10-19T14:03:02Z","updated_at":"2024-11-03T14:26:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/56244","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56244","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56244.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56244.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"The docstring for `filesize` failes to mention how paths are joined, and document the full-path-as-single-argument method. I know that this version of the docstring might be a regression when it comes to accurately reflecting the underlying methods, but it does serve the propose of documenting how it should be called much better, which is IMO the purpose of docstrings.

I also updated the docstring for `stat` to drop the shortcut, and be more explicit about the intended format for arguments to be slurped and stating that `joinpath` is used.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56244/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56244/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2453446874","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56244#issuecomment-2453446874","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56244","id":2453446874,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6SPKDa","user":{"login":"KronosTheLate","id":61620837,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxNjIwODM3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61620837?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate","html_url":"https://github.com/KronosTheLate","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-03T14:26:24Z","updated_at":"2024-11-03T14:26:24Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> We should probably keep filesize in sync with all of ...

That turned out to be a bigger job than I expected. I ended up putting in the work to clean up the docstrings throughout the module. Here is a highlight of the changes made, and relevant desicions:
- I changed `file` to `path` in all docstrings where the intended usage is passing a path
- I added signatures for `stat_struct`, which replaces the signatures that take in `file`, as it is more explicit on how to pass in a "file". This can confuse newer users, but felt important to accurately and completely document potential usage of the function. This signature comes last to minimize that potential confusion.
  - I did _not_ add signatures for `stat_struct` for functions that are clearly intended to operate on paths rather than file objects/descriptors, namely the functions `isfile`, `ispath`, `isdir`, `issocket`, and `islink`. I figured it would only add confusion, and that for these functions, the method that take `StatStruct`s as inputs are best considered an implementation detail rather than valid usage of the function.
- I cleaned up the language in terms of accuracy, such as changing "Return `true` if `path` is a symbolic link" into "Return `true` if `path` points to a symbolic link". The original version was plain wrong when taken literally, as `path` is a string and not a symbolic link. This makes the language a bit more verbose, but makes it so that when you read each word carefully you get the right conclusion, which was impossible before without also making the right assumptions.
- The original PR mentions the internal usage of `joinpath` in the docstring. Adding this to each and every docstring felt like too much, and the `functionname(path_elements...)` signature shoud document this sufficiently. I have kept the explicit statement "If multiple arguments are given, they are joined by [`joinpath`](@ref)" in the docstring for `stat`, so that if a user keeps drilling down, they will reach the `stat` function and can read it there. Adding the `stat_struct` signatures, along with the new and improved docstring for `StatStruct`, makes it more possible to understand that `stat` is used internally in all cases, which is why it felt adequate to mention `joinpath` explicitly only once, and in the docstring for `stat`
- At least one punctuation, and one plural-replated gramatical error.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2453446874/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

 """
     issetgid(path) -> Bool

+    issetgid(stat_struct) -> Bool

-Return `true` if `path` is a symbolic link, `false` otherwise.
+Return `true` if `path` points to a symbolic link, `false` otherwise.
 """
 islink(st::StatStruct) = filemode(st) & 0xf000 == 0xa000
 
 """
     issocket(path) -> Bool
+    issocket(path_elements...) -> Bool
 
-Return `true` if `path` is a socket, `false` otherwise.
+Return `true` if `path` points to a socket, `false` otherwise.
 """
 issocket(st::StatStruct) = filemode(st) & 0xf000 == 0xc000
 
 # mode permission predicates
 
 """
     issetuid(path) -> Bool
+    issetuid(path_elements...) -> Bool
+    issetuid(stat_struct) -> Bool
 
-Return `true` if `path` has the setuid flag set, `false` otherwise.
+Return `true` if the file at `path` or file descriptor `stat_struct` have the setuid flag set, `false` otherwise.
 """
 issetuid(st::StatStruct) = (filemode(st) & 0o4000) > 0
 
 """
     issetgid(path) -> Bool
+    issetgid(path_elements...) -> Bool
+    issetgid(stat_struct) -> Bool
 
-Return `true` if `path` has the setgid flag set, `false` otherwise.
+Return `true` if the file at `path` or file descriptor `stat_struct` have the setgid flag set, `false` otherwise.
 """
 issetgid(st::StatStruct) = (filemode(st) & 0o2000) > 0
 
 """
     issticky(path) -> Bool
+    issticky(path_elements...) -> Bool
+    issticky(stat_struct) -> Bool
 
-Return `true` if `path` has the sticky bit set, `false` otherwise.
+Return `true` if the file at `path` or file descriptor `stat_struct` have the sticky bit set, `false` otherwise.
 """
 issticky(st::StatStruct) = (filemode(st) & 0o1000) > 0

+    uperm(stat_struct)
 
-Get the permissions of the owner of the file as a bitfield of
+Return a bitfield of the owner permissions for the file at `path` or file descriptor `stat_struct`.
 
 | Value | Description        |
 |:------|:-------------------|
 | 01    | Execute Permission |
 | 02    | Write Permission   |
 | 04    | Read Permission    |

+The fact that a bitfield is returned means that if the permission
+is read+write, the bitfield is "110", which maps to the decimal
+value of 0+2+4=6. This is reflected in the printing of the
+returned `UInt8` value.
+
+See also [`gperm`](@ref) and [`operm`](@ref).

documentation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-02-23T14:33:39Z","updated_at":"2024-11-26T11:41:57Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I will not make a PR about this, because I just learnt about this today.

Given a poorly conditioned problem `Ax=b`, where the problem is to find x, the standard solution method `A\b` can give rubbish results.
Using the SVD of `A` to compute the solution is more numerically stable (or something else that makes it work better, not 100%).
One can get the SVD solution by `svd(A)\b`. This usage of an object of type `SVD` is not in the docstring for `SVD` not `svd`. I think that examples should be included, to showcase this way of using an `SVD` object.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/914/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/914/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

functionality"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-02-02T13:10:07Z","updated_at":"2024-03-05T08:28:41Z","closed_at":"2024-02-29T18:01:48Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/53159","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53159","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53159.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53159.patch","merged_at":"2024-02-29T18:01:47Z"},"body":"This PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation was inspired by [this comment](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795/3?u=thelatekronos), and therefore had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. I opted against this behaviour, because it fails to respect that an unbuffered channel is always full, in two important senses:
    1) The number of elements available is greater than or equal the capacity
    2) A call to `put!` will block

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in [docs-reference](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/parallel/)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53159/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53159/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1978206737","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53159#issuecomment-1978206737","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53159","id":1978206737,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM516QoR","user":{"login":"KronosTheLate","id":61620837,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxNjIwODM3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61620837?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate","html_url":"https://github.com/KronosTheLate","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-03-05T08:28:39Z","updated_at":"2024-03-05T08:28:39Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I would not find the changes made here in the 1.11 alpha release notes. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual

Fair point. I expect that many people who regularly work with matrices, which are a lot of people, would feel the same. Importantly, people who use Julia are more likely to also be people who regularly work with matrices. So I expect this will be an issue for others as well.

I do however have to object. The opposite of full is empty, not sparse. Full/empty is a more natural dichotomy than full/sparse. The current name has the most natural meaning, without being so specific as to become hard to discover. Also, would the opposite of sparse not be dense? It might need some locks and/or atomics, but yes, that sounds fine (and very similar to https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720)

So that PR has been stale since mid-2021. Particularly relevant is perhaps Jeff's comment:
> These functions seem like a bad idea to me --- don't they just encourage writing race conditions?

I would clearly like to have this implemented, and it seems like you both are also not against it. What is the appropriate path forward? I have to say that the names proposed in #40720 seem quite bad to me, especially when `isfull` is available. I am unsure if it is most appropriate to change that PR, make a new one, or wait until someone with merge rights just "gets it done" (It seems like things progress somewhat faster when the core devs are the only ones involved, which is of course completely fair).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1920959456/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","number":53159,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/53159","id":1708419973,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5l1GuF","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53159","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53159.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53159.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53159","number":53159,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Implement and export `isfull`","user":{"login":"KronosTheLate","id":61620837,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxNjIwODM3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61620837?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate","html_url":"https://github.com/KronosTheLate","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. This had two major flaws:
1) It became much harder to explain what the function does in a generic sense, as it did conceptually did different things for buffered and unbuffered channels. Which made me realize: If I am having a hard time explaining it, it will be harder to understand and thereby use it.
2) In fact, it made `isfull` behave more like `isready`, in that it returned true whenever a value was available. We already have `isready`

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in `isfull`","user":{"login":"KronosTheLate","id":61620837,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxNjIwODM3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61620837?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate","html_url":"https://github.com/KronosTheLate","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-02-02T13:10:07Z","updated_at":"2024-02-02T13:20:33Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/53159","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53159","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53159.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53159.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. This had two major flaws:
1) It became much harder to explain what the function does in a generic sense, as it did conceptually did different things for buffered and unbuffered channels. Which made me realize: If I am having a hard time explaining it, it will be harder to understand and thereby use it.
2) In fact, it made `isfull` behave more like `isready`, in that it returned true whenever a value was available. We already have `isready`

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in [docs-reference](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/parallel/)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53159/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53159/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1923791913","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53159#issuecomment-1923791913","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53159","id":1923791913,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5yqrwp","user":{"login":"KronosTheLate","id":61620837,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxNjIwODM3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61620837?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate","html_url":"https://github.com/KronosTheLate","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-02-02T13:20:33Z","updated_at":"2024-02-02T13:20:33Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"If {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1479913082","pull_request_review_id":1865364578,"id":1479913082,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5YNa56","diff_hunk":"@@ -543,6 +543,43 @@ function n_avail(c::Channel)
     @atomic :monotonic c.n_avail_items
 end
 
+"""
+    isfull(c::Channel)
+
+Determines if a [`Channel`](@ref) is full, in the
+sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.
+Returns immediately, does not block.
+
+Note that if another task is currently waiting to `take!`
+a value from `c`, a call to `put!(c, some_value)` might
+immediately be unblocked, even if `isfull(c)==true`.
+This is particularly relevant for unbuffered channels.","path":"base/channels.jl","commit_id":"4c8d252463d5d964341a0499ab1f03d816c0e12c","original_commit_id":"4c8d252463d5d964341a0499ab1f03d816c0e12c","user":{"login":"KronosTheLate","id":61620837,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxNjIwODM3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61620837?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate","html_url":"https://github.com/KronosTheLate","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"While this looks like a good warning, it seems odd to me to describe the function as "test if full in the sense that `put` will block", and in the next sentence say that "maybie frequently this will ont block when returning true". If I read that, I would be left with a feeling of "okay, so which one is it". In that sense I like my original version, that just explains a case in which it might go wrong, and leaves it to the user to use that knowledge.

But the warning could be harder. What about
> Note that a call to `isfull` generally occurs in a different instant than any call to `put!`. In particular, calling `isfull` and then subsequently `put!` guarantees that the calls occur at different instants. Therefore, the state of the channel may change between the calls, and generally `isfull` can not safely be used to see if a call to `put!` will in fact block.

A little verbose, but very clear and understandable and export `isfull`","user":{"login":"KronosTheLate","id":61620837,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxNjIwODM3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61620837?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate","html_url":"https://github.com/KronosTheLate","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation was inspired by [this comment](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795/3?u=thelatekronos), and therefore had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. I opted against this behaviour, because it fails to respect that an unbuffered channel is always full, in two important senses:
    1) The number of elements available is greater than or equal the capacity
    2) A call to `put!` will block

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation was inspired by [this comment](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795/3?u=thelatekronos), and therefore had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. I opted against this behaviour, because it fails to respect that an unbuffered channel is always full, in two important senses:
    1) The number of elements available is greater than or equal the capacity
    2) A call to `put!` will block

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation was inspired by [this comment](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795/3?u=thelatekronos), and therefore had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. I opted against this behaviour, because it fails to respect that an unbuffered channel is always full, in two important senses:
    1) The number of elements available is greater than or equal the capacity
    2) A call to `put!` will block

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation was inspired by [this comment](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795/3?u=thelatekronos), and therefore had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. I opted against this behaviour, because it fails to respect that an unbuffered channel is always full, in two important senses:
    1) The number of elements available is greater than or equal the capacity
    2) A call to `put!` will block

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in      @atomic :monotonic c.n_avail_items
 end
 
+"""
+    isfull(c::Channel)
+
+Determines if a [`Channel`](@ref) is full, in the
+sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.
+Returns immediately, does not block.
+
+Note that if another task is currently waiting to `take!`
+a value from `c`, a call to `put!(c, some_value)` might
+immediately be unblocked, even if `isfull(c)==true`.
+This is particularly relevant for unbuffered channels.","path":"base/channels.jl","commit_id":"d89b4140d87dcb23e3acc6e0988a1f199409c1f6","original_commit_id":"4c8d252463d5d964341a0499ab1f03d816c0e12c","user":{"login":"KronosTheLate","id":61620837,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxNjIwODM3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61620837?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate","html_url":"https://github.com/KronosTheLate","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I ment to say a different point in time. PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation was inspired by [this comment](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795/3?u=thelatekronos), and therefore had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. I opted against this behaviour, because it fails to respect that an unbuffered channel is always full, in two important senses:
    1) The number of elements available is greater than or equal the capacity
    2) A call to `put!` will block

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation was inspired by [this comment](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795/3?u=thelatekronos), and therefore had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. I opted against this behaviour, because it fails to respect that an unbuffered channel is always full, in two important senses:
    1) The number of elements available is greater than or equal the capacity
    2) A call to `put!` will block

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1480039965","pull_request_review_id":1865563410,"id":1480039965,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5YN54d","diff_hunk":"@@ -543,6 +543,43 @@ function n_avail(c::Channel)
     @atomic :monotonic c.n_avail_items
 end
 
+"""
+    isfull(c::Channel)
+
+Determines if a [`Channel`](@ref) is full, in the
+sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.
+Returns immediately, does not block.
+
+Note that if another task is currently waiting to `take!`
+a value from `c`, a call to `put!(c, some_value)` might
+immediately be unblocked, even if `isfull(c)==true`.
+This is particularly relevant for unbuffered channels.","path":"base/channels.jl","commit_id":"d89b4140d87dcb23e3acc6e0988a1f199409c1f6","original_commit_id":"4c8d252463d5d964341a0499ab1f03d816c0e12c","user":{"login":"KronosTheLate","id":61620837,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxNjIwODM3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61620837?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate","html_url":"https://github.com/KronosTheLate","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"> In concurrent code with channels, "a point in time" is not a concept that exists

I mean, surely if I start a very accurate watch outside the computer, there is a point in time in which it is decided if the return value is true or false, right? And that clock could form a reference between tasks, which would determine the sequence in which events happen between the tasks? It at least makes inntuitive sense that any event must have a temporal coordinate, just like any object must have a spatial position. But perhaps the issue is how well-defined that point is, like the exact position of a gas cloud?

> "By calling isfull, the caller can establish a happens-before relationship between a prior state of the channel and the future state after a put! call on the current thread. By putting a lock on the channel around two calls, or by guaranteeing there is a single producer, the caller can use this to create blocking-free code. However note that this may create livelocks, which may be worse."

This is not really readable to me, and I find it hard to turn that explanation into an understanding of what goes on, or what I can/can not do...

What about a more generic warning:
> Note that the state of a channel can change between calls to `isfull` and `put!`, meaning that there is no general and safe way to use the two functions to guarantee that a call to `put!` will or will not actually PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation was inspired by [this comment](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795/3?u=thelatekronos), and therefore had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. I opted against this behaviour, because it fails to respect that an unbuffered channel is always full, in two important senses:
    1) The number of elements available is greater than or equal the capacity
    2) A call to `put!` will block

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation was inspired by [this comment](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795/3?u=thelatekronos), and therefore had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. I opted against this behaviour, because it fails to respect that an unbuffered channel is always full, in two important senses:
    1) The number of elements available is greater than or equal the capacity
    2) A call to `put!` will block

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in      @atomic :monotonic c.n_avail_items
 end
 
+"""
+    isfull(c::Channel)
+
+Determines if a [`Channel`](@ref) is full, in the
+sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.
+Returns immediately, does not block.
+
+Note that if another task is currently waiting to `take!`
+a value from `c`, a call to `put!(c, some_value)` might
+immediately be unblocked, even if `isfull(c)==true`.
+This is particularly relevant for unbuffered channels.","path":"base/channels.jl","commit_id":"d89b4140d87dcb23e3acc6e0988a1f199409c1f6","original_commit_id":"4c8d252463d5d964341a0499ab1f03d816c0e12c","user":{"login":"KronosTheLate","id":61620837,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxNjIwODM3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61620837?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate","html_url":"https://github.com/KronosTheLate","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I just read my latest suggestion again, and I like it a lot. PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation was inspired by [this comment](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795/3?u=thelatekronos), and therefore had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. I opted against this behaviour, because it fails to respect that an unbuffered channel is always full, in two important senses:
    1) The number of elements available is greater than or equal the capacity
    2) A call to `put!` will block

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation was inspired by [this comment](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795/3?u=thelatekronos), and therefore had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. I opted against this behaviour, because it fails to respect that an unbuffered channel is always full, in two important senses:
    1) The number of elements available is greater than or equal the capacity
    2) A call to `put!` will block

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in      @atomic :monotonic c.n_avail_items
 end
 
+"""
+    isfull(c::Channel)
+
+Determines if a [`Channel`](@ref) is full, in the
+sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.
+Returns immediately, does not block.
+
+Note that if another task is currently waiting to `take!`
+a value from `c`, a call to `put!(c, some_value)` might
+immediately be unblocked, even if `isfull(c)==true`.
+This is particularly relevant for unbuffered channels.","path":"base/channels.jl","commit_id":"d89b4140d87dcb23e3acc6e0988a1f199409c1f6","original_commit_id":"4c8d252463d5d964341a0499ab1f03d816c0e12c","user":{"login":"KronosTheLate","id":61620837,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxNjIwODM3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61620837?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate","html_url":"https://github.com/KronosTheLate","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I am surely in over my head regarding the details you are describing. PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation was inspired by [this comment](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795/3?u=thelatekronos), and therefore had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. I opted against this behaviour, because it fails to respect that an unbuffered channel is always full, in two important senses:
    1) The number of elements available is greater than or equal the capacity
    2) A call to `put!` will block

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation was inspired by [this comment](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795/3?u=thelatekronos), and therefore had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. I opted against this behaviour, because it fails to respect that an unbuffered channel is always full, in two important senses:
    1) The number of elements available is greater than or equal the capacity
    2) A call to `put!` will block

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in      @atomic :monotonic c.n_avail_items
 end
 
+"""
+    isfull(c::Channel)
+
+Determines if a [`Channel`](@ref) is full, in the
+sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.
+Returns immediately, does not block.
+
+Note that if another task is currently waiting to `take!`
+a value from `c`, a call to `put!(c, some_value)` might
+immediately be unblocked, even if `isfull(c)==true`.
+This is particularly relevant for unbuffered channels.","path":"base/channels.jl","commit_id":"d89b4140d87dcb23e3acc6e0988a1f199409c1f6","original_commit_id":"4c8d252463d5d964341a0499ab1f03d816c0e12c","user":{"login":"KronosTheLate","id":61620837,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxNjIwODM3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61620837?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate","html_url":"https://github.com/KronosTheLate","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"A minor tweak to you original proposal:
```
Note that it may be frequently the case that `put!` will not block after this returns
`true`. Users must take precautions not to accidentally create live-lock bugs in their code
by calling this method, as these are generally harder to debug than deadlocks. It is also
possible that `put!` will block after this call returns `false`, if there are multiple producer
tasks calling `put!` in parallel.
```

I just aded "returns `false`" in the final sentence. But that is looks grat by me, specially concidering how far into the weeds the concersation goes when I try to be precise. PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation was inspired by [this comment](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795/3?u=thelatekronos), and therefore had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. I opted against this behaviour, because it fails to respect that an unbuffered channel is always full, in two important senses:
    1) The number of elements available is greater than or equal the capacity
    2) A call to `put!` will block

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation was inspired by [this comment](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795/3?u=thelatekronos), and therefore had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. I opted against this behaviour, because it fails to respect that an unbuffered channel is always full, in two important senses:
    1) The number of elements available is greater than or equal the capacity
    2) A call to `put!` will block

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation was inspired by [this comment](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795/3?u=thelatekronos), and therefore had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. I opted against this behaviour, because it fails to respect that an unbuffered channel is always full, in two important senses:
    1) The number of elements available is greater than or equal the capacity
    2) A call to `put!` will block

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in call"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":65,"created_at":"2021-01-02T13:08:04Z","updated_at":"2024-01-03T13:30:28Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/39071","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39071","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39071.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39071.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"I think it would be useful to have an easy way to make a range of points spaced logarithmically. This PR adds the following:
```julia
julia> LogRange(0.03, 810, 7)  # after some updates
7-element LogRange{Float64}:
 0.03, 0.164317, 0.9, 4.9295, 27.0, 147.885, 810.0

julia> LogRange(0.03, 27, 5) |> collect
5-element Vector{Float64}:
  0.03
  0.16431676725154984
  0.9
  4.929503017546495
 27.0
```
Before Julia 1.0, there was a `logspace` function which did something different, and less useful:
```julia
julia> logspace(1, 4, 4, base=2)
4-element Array{Float64,1}:
  2.0
  4.0
  8.0
 16.0
```
This was removed in #25896 in favour of just writing `2 .^ (1:4)`. If you know what *powers* you want, that is fine. But if you know what *start and endpoints* you want, then it's more of a hassle to write something like `2 .^ range(log2(2), log2(16), length=4)`. Well, for powers of 2, I think we can all do that in our heads, but if you want 20 plot points between about 3.5 and 1700, that's when this would be neat. 

After deciding whether or not this is a good idea at all:

* ~~I've given this the same notation as #39069 but it could equally well be `logrange(lo, hi; length)` instead, or in addition.~~
* Matlab's `logspace` behaves like the old one, so perhaps best to avoid that name. As @mkitti points out below, Python also has similar `logspace`, and a `geomspace` which is like this. That's another possible name. Mathematica's `PowerRange[a,b,r]` takes start, stop, and ratio instead. (This one could be upgraded to accept keywords `ratio=1.1` instead of `length=10`, like `range`, but I'm not sure that's often useful.)
* ~~Ideally it would use something like TwicePrecision to hit the endpoints more precisely? If it is to be an iterator, then maybe it should iterate by multiplication, not by addition & `exp`.~~

### Edit:

The current implementation is a `LogRange` struct. Like `LinRange`, this should hit the endpoints exactly, but not always rational intermediate points. I think this is a reasonable trade-off, and matches what was asked succinctly [here](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39071#issuecomment-756407033).

This thread got quite long, sorry. There are about a dozen implementations (here and in a linked gist), many trying harder to hit intermediate points, trying to figure out what was possible. Too many examples also show powers of 10, as that's an eay place to see how often you hit the exact number... these are a distraction, I think.

Perhaps I should also stress that the definition of this is mathematically very simple. Just as `LinRange(lo, hi, 3)` is the two ends plus their arithmetic mean, so `LogRange(lo, hi, 3)` is the two ends plus the geometric mean. For any quantity which is commonly plotted on a log scale, or measured in dB, this is the natural way to divide up the space between `lo` and `hi`. No mention of a base is necessary. No magic inference about whether `0.1` really means `1//10` is needed either.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/39071/reactions","total_count":26,"+1":19,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":7,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/39071/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1875375451","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/39071#issuecomment-1875375451","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/39071","id":1875375451,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5vx_Vb","user":{"login":"KronosTheLate","id":61620837,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxNjIwODM3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61620837?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate","html_url":"https://github.com/KronosTheLate","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-01-03T13:30:27Z","updated_at":"2024-01-03T13:30:27Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":">  In an ideal world, neither LinRange nor LogRange would be in Base

was about to make a PR to add `isfull` for channels, as I found myself implementing it for a project. It was the first and only project where I used channels, which I think says something about how useful and natural such functionality was.

There is also a [relevant discourse discussion](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795). Between me about to make a PR, that discourse post, and this issue, I would say that this is worth re-visiting. The implementation suggested in the discourse post is:
```julia
begin isfull(ch::Channel) =
    if ch.sz_max===0
        isready(ch)
    else
        length(ch.data) ≥ ch.sz_max
    end
end
```

My proposed implementation, relying on other definitions, is as follows:
```julia
isfull(c::Channel) = isbuffered(c) ? (Base.n_avail(c) == c.sz_max) : isready(c)
```
Note that "Base.n_avail is a "Lock-free equivalent to `length(c.data) + length(c.cond_put.waitq)`", meaning that the proposals are not exactly equivalent. I am not sure about which is better in terms of behaviour, as I do not know in which cases `length(c.cond_put.waitq` would make a difference.

Regarding @StefanKarpinski note about `isfull` not being an ideal name, I need a little more convincing. I currently find that that name is very natural and therefore discoverable, nicely mirrors `isempty` (which is defined for `Channel`), and is easy to read and understand the meaning of. I also do not know about good alternatives.

Note that DataStructures exports `isfull`, so a compatability-PR should be made there should this go through.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1919211960/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Essentially leveraging pkgimages to move as many stdlibs out of the base system image and allow upgradeability without requiring Julia upgrades.

Currently the only upgradeable stdlib is `DelimitedFiles`. And maybe soon (tm) `Statistics` #46501 

@KristofferC recently described to me on Slack the steps necessary to make a stdlib upgradeable?

The steps for an upgradable stdlib are:
    1. Move the stdlib to an external repo:
    2. Remove the stdlib from the sysimage
    3. Make the version of the stdlib to the same as the current Julia version
    4. Run https://github.com/KristofferC/StdlibRegistryCompatUpdater/blob/d79d66f7734e3d8d71213a4cf6250bea84dafe43/src/StdlibRegistryCompatUpdater.jl#L40 with the stdlib name and UUID to generate a PR to the registry equivalent to https://github.com/JuliaRegistries/General/pull/66039
   5. do something like https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pull/3373

For Step `2.` one can look at `pkgimage.mk` if the stdlib has a pkgbuilder defined for it. (x-ref: #50684)

Would be great if we could crowd-source this process!


```[tasklist]
### Tasks
- [x] DelimitedFiles.jl #45540 #48671
- [x] Statistics.jl #46501
- [ ] Distributed  https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51621
- [ ] SparseArrays
- [ ] LazyArtifacts https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51549
- [ ] SharedArrays
- [ ] Test https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51632
- [ ] Pkg.jl https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pull/3638
- [ ] LinearAlgebra https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51633
- [ ] Random https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51432
- [ ] FileWatching https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51463
- [ ] Require compat annotations in registry https://github.com/JuliaRegistries/RegistryCI.jl/pull/516
- [ ] ...
```

### How to split a stdlib into a seperate repository

contrib/excise_stdlib.sh Test

Essentially leveraging pkgimages to move as many stdlibs out of the base system image and allow upgradeability without requiring Julia upgrades.

Currently the only upgradeable stdlib is `DelimitedFiles`. And maybe soon (tm) `Statistics` #46501 

@KristofferC recently described to me on Slack the steps necessary to make a stdlib upgradeable?

The steps for an upgradable stdlib are:
    1. Move the stdlib to an external repo:
    2. Remove the stdlib from the sysimage
    3. Make the version of the stdlib to the same as the current Julia version
    4. Run https://github.com/KristofferC/StdlibRegistryCompatUpdater/blob/d79d66f7734e3d8d71213a4cf6250bea84dafe43/src/StdlibRegistryCompatUpdater.jl#L40 with the stdlib name and UUID to generate a PR to the registry equivalent to https://github.com/JuliaRegistries/General/pull/66039
   5. do something like https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pull/3373

For Step `2.` one can look at `pkgimage.mk` if the stdlib has a pkgbuilder defined for it. (x-ref: #50684)

Would be great if we could crowd-source this process!


```[tasklist]
### Tasks
- [x] DelimitedFiles.jl #45540 #48671
- [x] Statistics.jl #46501
- [ ] Distributed  https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51621
- [ ] SparseArrays
- [ ] LazyArtifacts https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51549
- [ ] SharedArrays
- [ ] Test https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51632
- [ ] Pkg.jl https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pull/3638
- [ ] LinearAlgebra https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51633
- [ ] Random https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51432
- [ ] FileWatching https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51463
- [ ] Require compat annotations in registry https://github.com/JuliaRegistries/RegistryCI.jl/pull/516
- [ ] ...
```

### How to split a stdlib into a seperate repository

```
contrib/excise_stdlib.sh Test

sincpi, coscpi, sincu, coscu, and kwargs to sinc, cosc","user":{"login":"moble","id":1470769,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE0NzA3Njk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1470769?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/moble","html_url":"https://github.com/moble","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/moble/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/moble/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/moble/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/moble/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/moble/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/moble/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/moble/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/moble/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/moble/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-10-02T18:37:35Z","updated_at":"2023-10-02T19:55:55Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/51551","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51551","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51551.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51551.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"The commenters on [this discourse post](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/unnormalized-sinc/25015) and [this github issue](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/36954#issuecomment-833020887) seemed to agree that some thing needed to be done about the `sinc` and `cosc` functions related to the choices of normalization.  Specifically, I think this sums up the suggestions in those threads:

  1. Add a keyword argument `normalized=true` to `sinc`.
  2. Introduce `sincpi` to return the normalized `sinc` function, and to rhyme with `sincpi`, `cospi`, etc.
  3. Introduce `sincu` to return the unnormalized `sinc` function.
  4. Add a keyword argument `normalized=true` to `cosc`
  5. Introduce `coscpi`
  6. Introduce `coscu`
  7. Add documentation suggesting that the explicit forms should be used, to pave the way for deprecation in the future. 

This PR does all of those *except* the last one, as I'm not sure how likely it is that such a thing will ever happen.  I welcome input on that, or any other issue.

@stevengj @PallHaraldsson @KronosTheLate ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51551/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51551/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1743666121","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51551#issuecomment-1743666121","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51551","id":1743666121,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5n7jvJ","user":{"login":"KronosTheLate","id":61620837,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYxNjIwODM3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/61620837?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate","html_url":"https://github.com/KronosTheLate","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KronosTheLate/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-10-02T19:55:55Z","updated_at":"2023-10-02T19:55:55Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"This solution seems to fix most of the problem in a non-breaking way that makes a lot of sense. They take up 68, 143 and 449 MB. That is a lot for files that end up as trash lying around on my system.

They all come from the same day, and I believe they were created at a point when I got mad at the amount of RAM and SWAP memory hogged by background Julia processes started by VSCode, and i force-closed them.

My issue is that these potentially huge dumps should not be produced in a hidden place, cluttering the disk of users.

This issue may be more appropriately aimed at the VSCode extension, in which case I will copy the issue-text  in a new issue over there.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51722/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51722/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}
